title: Home to Harlem
description: Home to Harlem — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: 125th Street
memes.description: A major east–west thoroughfare in Harlem, home to a German delicatessen frequented by characters in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem. (017-010)

memes.label: 130th Street
memes.description: An east-west thoroughfare in Harlem, Manhattan — a transitional corridor between the commercial spine of 125th Street and the residential blocks to the north, notable in Home to Harlem as a route of movement and a boundary within the neighborhood's social geography

memes.label: 135th Street
memes.description: A major Harlem cross-street that in Home to Harlem serves as the threshold of Jake's homecoming, a key axis of the neighborhood's nightlife geography, and the romantic rendezvous point of the novel's final pages

memes.label: 137th Street
memes.description: A street in Harlem featuring a buffet flat where Jake is taken by a companion, depicted in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem as a site of gambling, drinking, and dancing behind carefully shaded windows.

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "Home to Harlem" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: "Colophon"
memes.description: "Publication and production details"

memes.label: "Endnotes"
memes.description: "Endnotes for Home to Harlem by Claude McKay"

memes.description: "Front matter for Home to Harlem by Claude McKay (1928)"

memes.description: "Copyright and edition information for Home to Harlem"

memes.description: "Complete chapter listing for Home to Harlem by Claude McKay (1928)"

memes.label: "Uncopyright"
memes.description: "CC0 Public Domain Dedication"

memes.label: Africa
memes.description: The ancestral continent referenced throughout Home to Harlem as homeland, cultural origin, and a contested idea — imagined by Jake as primitive "jungle" but defended by Ray the Haitian waiter as a land of civilization, history, and cultural pride.

memes.label: Agatha
memes.description: A "rich-brown girl" who visits Ray in chapter 14 of Home to Harlem, acting wistfully as their relationship faces an uncertain future.

memes.label: Aida Overton Walker
memes.description: A celebrated African American vaudeville performer known as the "Queen of the Cakewalk," wife of George Walker, mentioned in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem. (006-051)(021-007)

memes.label: "Alabama"
memes.description: "U.S. state referenced in Home to Harlem in connection with character backgrounds and the Great Migration."

memes.label: Alphonse Daudet
memes.description: French novelist (1840–1897) whose 1884 novel Sapho is read and debated by the Haitian waiter and Jake Brown aboard the Pennsylvania Railroad dining car — functioning as the entry point for one of the novel's most significant discussions of literature, race, gender, and the politics of naming

memes.label: Amsterdam News
memes.description: A Negro newspaper read by characters in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem, one of the prominent Black newspapers of the Harlem Renaissance. (014-022)(015-025)

memes.label: Anita Patti Brown
memes.description: A celebrated Negro singer mentioned in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem alongside other prominent Black performers of the early twentieth century. (021-007)

memes.label: Armistice of 1918
memes.description: The November 11, 1918 ceasefire ending World War I — a historical hinge that finds Jake in London's East End, catalyzing the chain of postwar migration and desire that structures the opening chapters of Home to Harlem

memes.label: Asa Philip Randolph
memes.description: Prominent African American labor leader and civil rights activist who organized the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters, the first predominantly Black labor union to win a collective bargaining agreement

memes.label: Aunt Hattie
memes.description: A Harlem cookshop proprietress who represents the domestic, nurturing side of Black women's entrepreneurship — her chicken dinners and maternal warmth providing a counterpoint to the cabaret's commercialized pleasures

memes.label: Aunt Hattie's Cookshop
memes.description: A renowned basement chitterling joint and restaurant at 132nd Street in Harlem, celebrated in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem for its home-cooked Southern food and its role as a gathering place for the neighborhood's working-class Black residents.

memes.label: The Baltimore
memes.description: A cabaret and nightclub in Harlem that serves as one of the novel's central social spaces, where Jake meets his "little lost brown" and which is raided in chapter 8.

memes.label: Barrelhouse
memes.description: A type of rough, working-class drinking establishment in Prohibition-era Harlem and Pittsburgh — somewhere between a low-end speakeasy and an after-hours joint, frequented by laborers and open all night.

memes.label: "Barron's Cabaret"
memes.description: "A prominent Harlem cabaret and nightclub referenced in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem, part of the neighborhood's vibrant nightlife scene."

memes.label: Battle of Colored and White Men
memes.description: A large-scale racial battle in London's East End during the summer after the Armistice, witnessed by Jake Brown, which precipitated his lonesomeness and return to America.
aliases: Battle of Colored and White Men

memes.label: Bert Williams
memes.description: A real-life Black vaudeville star (1874–1922) referenced in Home to Harlem as a marker of the "sporting days" — representing the earlier generation of Black entertainers who paved the way for the Harlem Renaissance

memes.label: Billy Biasse
memes.description: A longshoreman friend of Jake's who runs a gambling joint in his railroad flat — a fixture of Harlem's working-class sporting world and a connector between the dock, the buffet flat, and the gambling table

memes.label: Black Belt
memes.description: The segregated Black neighborhood of Harlem — the geographic, social, and symbolic center of Black urban life in 1920s New York, used in Home to Harlem interchangeably with the term "the Belt" to designate the encircling community where Black characters live, work, and pursue pleasure

memes.label: The Blues
memes.description: The foundational African American musical form built on twelve-bar structure and blue notes — the emotional soundtrack of working-class Harlem in Home to Harlem, permeating every social space from cabarets to house parties

memes.label: Boarding House
memes.description: A residential building type common in 1920s Harlem where tenants rented individual rooms, often with shared facilities, serving as an affordable housing option for the neighborhood's working-class and transient population.

memes.label: Brest
memes.description: The French port city where Jake was stationed during WWI, a pivotal site in his memory that represents both the drudgery of segregated military labor and the liberatory possibilities of European racial attitudes

memes.label: Brooklyn
memes.description: A New York City borough referenced in Home to Harlem as an alternative to Harlem's nightlife, where a rival cabaret fails and where Susy and Zeddy's relationship plays out in domestic tension.

memes.label: "Brownskin"
memes.description: "A color descriptor and social category in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem, referring to brown-complexioned Black people within Harlem's color hierarchy."

memes.label: Buffet Flat
memes.description: A private apartment-turned-speakeasy in 1920s Harlem, combining gambling, drinking, dancing, and sometimes prostitution, run by enterprising women like Madame Suarez

memes.label: Bulldyker
memes.description: A period Harlem slang term for a masculine-presenting lesbian, appearing in a brief but significant exchange in Home to Harlem that reveals attitudes toward gender and sexuality in 1920s Black working-class culture

memes.label: Cabaret
memes.description: The defining institution of Harlem nightlife in the 1920s — nightclubs where Black entertainers performed for racially mixed audiences, where jazz and blues music flourished, and where the novel's characters socialize, fight, and fall in love.

memes.label: Cabaret Culture
memes.description: The vibrant public nightlife scene of 1920s Harlem, centered on Black-owned and operated cabarets that served as hubs of music, dance, social life, and cultural expression

memes.label: Cakewalk
memes.description: A historic African American dance form that originated on plantations in the nineteenth century, briefly referenced in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem when Jake prances as if doing "the old cakewalk."

memes.label: Chicago
memes.description: An Illinois city referenced at the end of Home to Harlem as a promised destination — a place of possibility that represents the next horizon for Harlem's restless characters.

memes.label: Claude McKay
memes.description: Jamaican-American poet, novelist, and leading figure of the Harlem Renaissance, author of Home to Harlem (1928).

memes.label: Cole and Johnson
memes.description: A pioneering African American songwriting and performing team of the early twentieth century, mentioned in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem among the greats of Black entertainment. (021-007)

memes.label: Colored Life
memes.description: A Negro newspaper whose sports editor appears at a fashionable Harlem gathering in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem. (021-009)

memes.label: The Congo
memes.description: A Harlem cabaret and nightclub that features prominently in Home to Harlem as a gathering place for the neighborhood's working-class nightlife and the venue where Congo Rose performs.

memes.label: Congo Rose
memes.description: The star entertainer at the Congo cabaret whose romantic relationship with Jake Brown is the novel's central love story — a figure of desire, talent, and heartbreak in Harlem's sporting life

memes.label: "Connor's Cabaret"
memes.description: "A Harlem cabaret referenced in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem as one of the enduring nightlife establishments of the era."

memes.label: Crime and Punishment
memes.description: Fyodor Dostoevsky's 1866 novel, read by Ray in *Home to Harlem* — a psychological study of guilt, redemption, and the moral consequences of transgression

memes.label: Dandy
memes.description: The figure of the dandy — the sharp-dressed, style-conscious Black man — as a mode of masculine self-presentation and social aspiration in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem.

memes.label: Dick Mulberry
memes.description: A promoter of Negro shows in early twentieth-century New York, husband of Madame Mulberry, mentioned in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem. (021-007)

memes.label: Dickty
memes.description: A Harlem slang term denoting high-class, snobbish, or uppity attitudes among Black people, often tied to color and class distinctions within the community

memes.label: Dining-Car
memes.description: The railroad dining car as a mobile workplace in Home to Harlem — a hierarchical, high-pressure environment where Jake works as a cook, where racial and ethnic tensions flare, and where transformative conversations occur between runs.

memes.label: Dixie Red
memes.description: The proprietor of a poolroom in Harlem that serves as a regular gathering place for Jake Brown, Billy Biasse, and other working-class men in Home to Harlem.

memes.label: Doc Giles
memes.description: A pre-war gambling house operator on Myrtle Avenue, mentioned by Strawberry Lips alongside Elijah Bowers as part of the lost golden age of Brooklyn Black nightlife before Harlem's ascendancy

memes.label: Draft Enforcement in Harlem
memes.description: The postwar campaign by police and military authorities to hunt down World War I draft dodgers and deserters in Harlem — a shadow conflict between Black communities and state enforcement

memes.label: East End
memes.description: The East End of London, a working-class district where Jake worked as a docker and found community before returning to Harlem in Claude McKay's *Home to Harlem* (001-024).

memes.label: Elevated Train
memes.description: The elevated railway ("El") that ran above Brooklyn and Manhattan streets, forming a distinctive part of New York City's 1920s transit infrastructure and soundscape

memes.label: Elijah Bowers
memes.description: A pre-war Harlem cabaret entrepreneur whose spectacular rise and fall — owning a grand automobile, killing a man in his cabaret, and being financially ruined — is narrated by Strawberry Lips as a morality tale of the nightlife economy

memes.label: "England"
memes.description: "Country referenced throughout Claude McKay's Home to Harlem as part of the transatlantic circuit of Black labor and migration during and after World War I."

memes.label: Express Subway
memes.description: The express subway train that Jake Brown takes from South Ferry to Harlem upon his arrival in New York City in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem. (002-002)

memes.label: Fairmount Park
memes.description: A large urban park in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, appearing in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem as a setting for leisurely encounters in the city's Black social world.

memes.label: A Farewell Feed
memes.description: Ray and Grant prepare to leave Harlem for a freighter journey across the Pacific — a farewell dinner at Aunt Hattie's, paid for by Billy Biasse, that distills the novel's tensions between home and departure, mind and body, Harlem's hold and the urge to escape.

memes.label: Fashion and Style
memes.description: The culture of self-presentation through clothing in Home to Harlem — suits, silk, the "nigger-brown" aesthetic, dandyism, and dress as a strategy of dignity and desire in 1920s Black Harlem

memes.label: Felice
memes.description: The "little lost brown of the Baltimore" — the woman Jake spends one night with and spends the entire novel seeking, whose reappearance in Chicago provides the novel's romantic resolution

memes.label: Fifteenth Street
memes.description: A street in Philadelphia where Jake and his companions visit a house, mentioned in the novel as the site of a social encounter in a city that stands in contrast to Harlem.

memes.label: Fifth Avenue
memes.description: A major thoroughfare in Harlem that serves as a commercial and residential artery in Home to Harlem, lined with rooming houses, shops, and restaurants.

memes.label: Fifty-third Street
memes.description: A historic hub of fashionable Negro life in early twentieth-century New York City, referenced in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem as a predecessor to Harlem's rise as the center of Black culture.

memes.label: Food and Dining
memes.description: The culinary landscape of Home to Harlem — from dining-car kitchens to Southern home cooking, food as cultural marker, economic necessity, and sensory pleasure across class and racial lines

memes.label: France
memes.description: France as a liberatory alternative to American racism in Home to Harlem — the wartime and postwar destination where Black soldiers and sailors experienced relative racial freedom, French colonial attitudes that contrasted with American segregation, and the port cities (Brest, Le Havre) that anchored the Black Atlantic circuit

memes.label: French Revolution
memes.description: The French Revolution (1789–1799) as a political idea in Home to Harlem — invoked as the ideological source of "Liberté, Egalité, Fraternité" and the inspiration for the Haitian Revolution, representing the universalist promise that even enslaved Black people could be "lifted up" by revolutionary ideals

memes.label: Poker and Gambling
memes.description: The culture of gambling — poker, craps, and poolroom betting — as a central feature of Black working-class male social life in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem.

memes.label: George Walker
memes.description: A vaudeville pioneer and dance man of the Williams and Walker duo, mentioned in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem as one of the great Black stage performers remembered by Harlem characters. (006-051)(021-007)

memes.label: Gertie Blake
memes.description: A woman in Rose's social circle in chapter 9 of Home to Harlem, mentioned in connection with a visit to Rose's room and the social life of the boarding house.

memes.label: Gin-head Susy
memes.description: A Harlem domestic worker whose all-male gin parties, obsessive preference for "yellow" men, and eventual relationship with Zeddy Plummer illuminate the gendered and color-conscious economy of Prohibition-era Black social life.

memes.label: Gin and Whisky
memes.description: The hard liquors that fueled Harlem's nightlife economy in Home to Harlem — gin for the working class, whisky for the aspiring, both illegal under Prohibition and central to social life

memes.label: "Goldgraben's"
memes.description: "A cabaret on Lenox Avenue referenced in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem, part of Harlem's vibrant Prohibition-era nightlife."

memes.label: Gordon Dry
memes.description: A brand of gin frequently referenced in Claude McKay's *Home to Harlem*, consumed by characters in Harlem's cabarets and social gatherings — invoked even as an oath of truthfulness (006-033).

memes.label: Grant
memes.description: A student and friend of Ray's who appears in Chapter XVI — a cynical, intellectual Negro who debates race and morality with Jake and Ray before shipping out on a freighter with Ray in Chapter XVIII

memes.label: The Great Migration
memes.description: The mass movement of African Americans from the rural South to Northern industrial cities (c. 1910–1970) — the demographic transformation that created the Harlem of Home to Harlem and shaped the lives of nearly every character in the novel

memes.label: Haitian Revolution
memes.description: The slave uprising that established Haiti as the first Black republic, discussed by characters in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem through the figure of Toussaint Louverture. (010-052)

memes.label: Harlem
memes.description: The Black cultural capital of 1920s America — the primary setting of Home to Harlem, where Lenox Avenue cabarets, buffet flats, and working-class social worlds form the novel's geographic and sensory core

memes.label: 369th Infantry Regiment (Harlem Hellfighters)
memes.description: The all-Black U.S. Army regiment that fought with distinction in WWI — whose band, led by James Reese Europe, introduced jazz to France and whose veterans populate Home to Harlem's social world

memes.label: Harlem Nightlife Economy
memes.description: The parallel economic system of Black-owned nightlife venues in Home to Harlem — from public cabarets to private buffet flats, gambling clubs, and bootleg liquor networks — that formed an autonomous Black economy operating alongside (and in tension with) white-dominated formal commerce.

memes.label: Harlem Renaissance
memes.description: The flourishing of African American literature, music, art, and intellectual life centered in 1920s Harlem — the cultural movement of which Claude McKay and Home to Harlem are defining (and controversial) expressions

memes.label: High-Brown Talc
memes.description: A cosmetic powder marketed to lighten the appearance of brown skin, mentioned in Home to Harlem's nightlife scenes as a detail that reveals the intersection of beauty culture and colorism in 1920s Harlem

memes.label: High-Yaller
memes.description: A color-complex slang term used throughout Home to Harlem for light-skinned Black people — indexing the hierarchies of colorism that structured desire, status, and identity in 1920s Harlem

memes.label: Home to Harlem
memes.description: Landmark 1928 novel by Claude McKay depicting working-class Black life in Harlem after World War I, the first novel by a Black writer to become a national bestseller.

memes.label: House Party
memes.description: A private social gathering in a Harlem apartment featuring music, drinking, and dancing — a domestic counterpart to public cabarets and an expression of working-class Black social life in Home to Harlem

memes.label: Jack Johnson
memes.description: The first Black heavyweight boxing champion (1908–1915), referenced in Home to Harlem as the ultimate symbol of Black masculine fame, physical prowess, and defiance of white supremacy — invoked by characters across the class spectrum as a benchmark of success and notoriety

memes.label: Jake Brown
memes.description: The protagonist of Claude McKay's Home to Harlem (1928) — a Black longshoreman, U.S. Army deserter, and sensual wanderer whose journey through Harlem's cabarets, buffet flats, docks, and railroad kitchens forms the novel's episodic backbone

memes.label: Jake's Desertion
memes.description: Jake Brown's decision to desert the United States Army in Brest, France after the Armistice, launching his journey back to Harlem

memes.label: "Jamaica"
memes.description: "Caribbean island nation referenced in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem as a source of West Indian immigrants to Harlem."

memes.label: James Reese Europe
memes.description: A real-life Black jazz pioneer and military bandleader (1880–1919) whose tragic death — stabbed by a drummer during a Boston performance — closes Home to Harlem on a note of historical elegy

memes.label: Jazz
memes.description: The revolutionary African American musical form that became the soundtrack of 1920s Harlem — an improvisational, syncopated force that permeated cabarets, buffet flats, and the sensory world of Home to Harlem

memes.label: Jerco
memes.description: A man who appears in chapter 17 of Home to Harlem, involved in a tragic love story with Rosalind that ends in his suicide.

memes.label: Langston Hughes
memes.description: American poet, novelist, playwright, and central figure of the Harlem Renaissance whose literary work celebrated Black working-class life and vernacular culture, contemporary with Claude McKay and the publication of Home to Harlem (1928).

memes.label: Le Havre
memes.description: French port city on the English Channel, referenced alongside Brest as a transit point for Black soldiers and workers moving between Europe and America during and after World War I

memes.label: Lenox Avenue
memes.description: "Harlem's primary commercial and social artery — the north-south avenue where cabarets, speakeasies, and the spectacle of Black urban modernity converge in Home to Harlem"

memes.label: "Leroy's Cabaret"
memes.description: "A celebrated Harlem cabaret referenced in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem, part of the neighborhood's legendary nightlife scene."

memes.label: Limehouse
memes.description: A historic district in London's East End, appearing in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem as the neighborhood where Jake Brown lived and worked as a docker during his time in England.

memes.label: Liver-lip
memes.description: A blue-black longshoreman in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem, nicknamed for his thick lips, who tells Jake about fighting strikebreakers with a razor.

memes.label: London
memes.description: London's East End dock district, a key node in Jake's transatlantic working circuit and the setting of the novel's opening chapter where Jake works, carouses, and eventually deserts to return to America.

memes.label: London East End
memes.description: The docklands district of London where Black soldiers and workers congregated during and after WWI — a key node in Jake's transatlantic working circuit and the novel's opening setting

memes.label: Longshoreman
memes.description: A dock worker who loads and unloads ships — Jake's primary occupation throughout Home to Harlem and a key to understanding his social position, physical identity, and access to Harlem's diverse social worlds

memes.label: Longshoremen's Strike
memes.description: Labor strike on the Brooklyn piers that tests Jake's principles against scabbing, and reveals the racial fault lines in early 20th-century American unionism.

memes.label: Ma Lawton
memes.description: The landlady of a boarding house in chapter 17 of Home to Harlem, who cares for her tenants Rosalind and Jerco and serves as a maternal figure in the neighborhood.

memes.label: Madame Jerkin
memes.description: The proprietress of "the niftiest buffet flat in Harlem" — a figure mentioned but not seen in Home to Harlem, representing the top tier of Harlem's informal nightlife entrepreneurship

memes.label: Madame Laura
memes.description: A Harlem woman who hosts a buffet flat party in Chapter XIII — the gathering where Jake brings Ray and where the evening culminates in a police raid — representing the informal nightlife entrepreneurship of Black women

memes.label: Madame Mulberry
memes.description: A famous Black beauty and socialite — once known as "Brown Glory" — who was a fixture of elite Harlem nightlife in the era when Fifty-third Street was the center of fashionable Negro society, wife of show promoter Dick Mulberry

memes.label: Madame Suarez
memes.description: The madame of a cabaret and buffet flat in Harlem catering to "sporty colored persons," raided by police in chapter 8 of Home to Harlem.

memes.label: Madame Suarez's Buffet Flat
memes.description: The most exclusive buffet flat in Harlem — run by Madame Adeline Suarez, catering to "sporty colored persons of consequence" and their white guests, representing the pinnacle of Prohibition-era private nightlife

memes.label: Madame Walker
memes.description: A wealthy Harlem salon proprietor in Claude McKay's *Home to Harlem*, known for her absinthe-colored salon and referenced as a symbol of affluence (007-088).

memes.label: Madison Avenue
memes.description: A major north-south avenue in Manhattan, briefly traversed by Jake and Felice in the final chapter of Home to Harlem as they walk from Madison Avenue through 130th Street toward the Block Beautiful

memes.label: "Maroon"
memes.description: "A color descriptor used in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem to characterize complexion and clothing, part of the novel's rich color vocabulary."

memes.label: Maunie Whitewing
memes.description: A scandalous beauty of Egyptian exoticism — wife of a nationally-known Negro artist, notorious across Brooklyn, New York, and Washington — who appears at the new cabaret in Chapter XXI, appraising Jake with a bold stare and a proposition in French

memes.label: Meeting of Jake and Rose at the Congo
memes.description: The meeting of Jake Brown and Congo Rose at the Congo cabaret in Harlem — the novel's central romantic encounter that sets the relationship plot in motion

memes.label: Migration
memes.description: The Great Migration context of Home to Harlem — the movement of Black Southerners and Caribbean immigrants to Northern cities, shaping the novel's character backgrounds, its opening transatlantic voyage, and the texture of Harlem as a migrant metropolis

memes.label: Mile End Road
memes.description: A street in London's East End where Jake celebrated New Year's Eve 1919 with friends in Claude McKay's *Home to Harlem* (001-025).

memes.label: Minstrelsy
memes.description: The American theatrical tradition of blackface minstrel shows, which created degrading racial stereotypes and against which African American artists and intellectuals of the Harlem Renaissance defined their cultural project

memes.label: Miss Curdy
memes.description: A friend and drinking companion of Susy in chapters 6–7 of Home to Harlem, known for her sharp tongue and social pretensions.

memes.label: "Monkey-Chaser"
memes.description: "A derogatory slang term used in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem for West Indian immigrants, reflecting intra-racial tensions in 1920s Harlem."

memes.label: Morocco
memes.description: A North African country that appeared in the imagination of early-twentieth-century African American writers and travelers as a site of cultural connection, colonial tension, and exotic fascination

memes.label: Muscatel
memes.description: A sweet fortified wine that appears as a recurring detail in Home to Harlem's nightlife scenes — the drink of choice at buffet flats and cabarets, signaling a specific stratum of Harlem's alcohol economy during Prohibition

memes.label: Myrtle Avenue
memes.description: A street in Harlem where Susy keeps her apartment near Prince Street, serving as a key setting for rent parties and domestic life in chapters 6–7 of Home to Harlem.

memes.label: New York City
memes.description: The metropolitan container of Harlem — the great port city whose docks, railroads, libraries, and social worlds form the broader urban setting of Home to Harlem, and against which Harlem defines itself as both part and apart

memes.label: One Night in Philly
memes.description: Jake and Ray spend a night at Madame Laura's house in Fifteenth Street, Philadelphia — a social world of Black pleasure, music, champagne, and police protection, where Ray's intellectual distance collides with Jake's easy immersion.

memes.label: Nije Gridley
memes.description: A longshoreman broker in Home to Harlem — the intermediary between dock workers and jobs, whose economic role and tall tales make him a representative figure of Harlem's informal labor economy

memes.label: Nile Queen
memes.description: An upscale restaurant on Seventh Avenue in Harlem, appearing in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem as a "niftier" dining spot where Jake and Felice celebrate.

memes.label: Numbers Game
memes.description: The illegal lottery — known as "policy" or "the numbers" — that was a pervasive economic institution in 1920s Harlem, operating as both a form of gambling and an informal Black banking system, though notably absent as an explicit presence in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem

memes.label: Ofay
memes.description: A Harlem slang term for white people, used by Black characters in Home to Harlem to mark racial boundaries, power dynamics, and moments of cross-racial encounter

memes.label: Palm Beach
memes.description: A Florida resort town where Felice, a character in Claude McKay's *Home to Harlem*, went to work after her brief encounter with Jake Brown (020-014).

memes.label: Panama
memes.description: A Central American country and the site of the Panama Canal, referenced in Home to Harlem in connection with Black migrant labor, Caribbean geography, and Ray's circuitous journey to the United States.

memes.label: "Part I"
memes.description: "Part I"

memes.label: "Part II"
memes.description: "Part II"

memes.label: "Part III"
memes.description: "Part III"

memes.label: Pennsylvania
memes.description: A U.S. state traversed by train in Home to Harlem, forming part of the railroad geography that structures the novel's second half and connects Harlem to the broader American landscape.

memes.label: Pennsylvania Railroad
memes.description: The major American railroad company — the largest in the United States by revenue in the early 20th century — that employed Jake and Ray as dining-car workers, providing the geographic mobility that structures the novel's mapping of Black urban networks

memes.label: Philadelphia
memes.description: A Pennsylvania city referenced in Home to Harlem as one of the East Coast ports and railroad stops where Jake finds community, gossip, and romantic encounters.

memes.label: Pittsburgh
memes.description: A Pennsylvania city that appears in chapter 11 of Home to Harlem as the setting for Jake's railroad layover, the "snowstorm in Pittsburgh" chapter.

memes.label: Pleasure-Seeking
memes.description: A central theme in Home to Harlem — the relentless pursuit of sensual pleasure (jazz, dancing, sex, drinking, gambling) as a response to the harshness of Black working-class life, organized through the "sporting world" and its nocturnal economy

memes.label: "Poker and Gambling"
memes.description: "The gambling culture of Harlem as depicted in Home to Harlem, centered on poker, dice, and betting activities that structure the social lives of Jake and his associates."

memes.label: Poolroom
memes.description: The poolroom as a central social institution for Black working-class men in 1920s Harlem, functioning as a gambling venue, gathering place, and masculine social sphere.

memes.label: Prince Street
memes.description: A street in Harlem near Myrtle Avenue, mentioned in the novel as a site of violence and rough neighborhood life, associated with Susy's apartment and the grittier edges of the working-class district.

memes.label: Prohibition
memes.description: The nationwide constitutional ban on alcohol production and sale (1920–1933) that created the underground economy driving all of Harlem's nightlife in Home to Harlem

memes.label: Pullman Company
memes.description: The company that manufactured and operated luxury railroad sleeping and dining cars, employing thousands of Black workers — including porters, cooks, and waiters — as the largest single employer of African Americans during the Great Migration era

memes.label: Pullman Porter
memes.description: An occupation central to African American labor history and the Great Migration, appearing in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem as a marker of Black working-class identity and mobility.

memes.label: Race and Color
memes.description: The novel's nuanced treatment of skin color as a social hierarchy within the Black community — McKay traces the spectrum from "spade" to "high-brown" to "yellow" and the interracial dynamics that shape Harlem's social world

memes.label: Ragtime
memes.description: A syncopated piano-based popular music style that preceded and overlapped with early jazz, mentioned in Home to Harlem as part of the musical texture of 1920s nightlife

memes.label: Raid of Madame Laura's Buffet Flat
memes.description: A white policeman's staged raid on Madame Laura's buffet flat in Philadelphia — a ritual of control that dissolves into a friendly drink, revealing the protection economy and the skilled negotiation of Black women nightlife operators during Prohibition.
aliases: Raid of Madame Laura's Buffet Flat

memes.label: Raid of the Baltimore
memes.description: The police closure of Harlem's premier cabaret — a pivotal event in Home to Harlem that reshapes the novel's nightlife geography, drives patrons from public cabarets into private buffet flats, and dramatizes the racial politics of Prohibition enforcement

memes.label: Railroad Flat
memes.description: A type of tenement apartment common in early twentieth-century Harlem, appearing in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem as the site of longshoremen's gambling gatherings.

memes.label: Ray
memes.description: A Haitian intellectual and former student who befriends Jake on the railroad — serving as the novel's voice of critique, education, and diasporic consciousness in counterpoint to Jake's physical, intuitive approach to life

memes.label: "Razor"
memes.description: "A weapon emblematic of masculine violence and conflict resolution in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem, appearing in street fights, cabaret brawls, and intimate disputes."

memes.label: Rent Party
memes.description: A Harlem social institution in which tenants hosted parties in their apartments, charging admission at the door to raise money for rent — an informal mutual-aid practice that sustained Black working-class tenants during the 1920s and formed part of the domestic nightlife economy documented in Home to Harlem

memes.label: Rosalind
memes.description: A woman living in Ma Lawton's boarding house whose illness and death in chapter 17 of Home to Harlem reveals the depth of her relationship with Jerco.

memes.label: Rose (Congo Rose)
memes.description: A cabaret singer at the Congo — Harlem's all-Black nightclub — who becomes Jake's lover, using her talents to extract money from men in a dynamic that embodies both the exploitation and agency of Black women in 1920s nightlife

memes.label: Sah Farrel
memes.description: A character in Claude McKay's *Home to Harlem*, appearing in Chapter XII as a figure of authority addressed in dialect as "Sah Farrel" by the kitchen staff (012-051).

memes.label: Saloon
memes.description: The saloon as a working-class social institution in 1920s Harlem, serving as a drinking establishment, informal gathering place, and key setting in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem.

memes.label: Sappho (Daudet)
memes.description: "Alphonse Daudet's 1884 novel Sappho: Parisian Manners — a French naturalist work about a young man's affair with a courtesan, read by Ray on the railroad as a marker of his European literary education and intellectual distance from Jake's vernacular world"

memes.label: Scabbing
memes.description: The practice of crossing a picket line to work during a strike — a charged moral issue in Home to Harlem that tests Jake's working-class ethics against economic necessity

memes.label: Seventh Avenue
memes.description: A major Harlem thoroughfare in Home to Harlem known for its promenading culture, trees, and role as a social stage for the neighborhood's fashionable residents.

memes.label: "Shine"
memes.description: "A Harlem slang term used in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem, appearing in the phrase 'dickty shines' to describe well-dressed, high-status Black people."

memes.label: Sleeping Late
memes.description: A recurring motif in Home to Harlem — the practice and pleasure of sleeping late, staying in bed, and keeping irregular hours, which marks characters' resistance to the work-discipline expected of Black working-class bodies and their embrace of a pleasure-centered life rhythm

memes.label: Snowstorm in Pittsburgh
memes.description: Jake's railroad layover in Pittsburgh — a night of physical hardship, urban desolation, cocaine, and existential crisis that tests both Jake's resilience and Ray's fragile hold on sanity.

memes.label: Social Hierarchy in Home to Harlem
memes.description: The system of social labels and class distinctions — dickty, ofay, high-yaller, sweetman, dandy, bulldyker, longshoreman — through which McKay maps Harlem's internal social stratification and the intersections of race, color, class, gender, and sexuality.

memes.label: "South Carolina"
memes.description: "U.S. state referenced in Home to Harlem as part of the Southern background of characters who migrated north to Harlem."

memes.label: South Ferry
memes.description: The southern Manhattan terminal where Jake Brown arrives after being paid off from his ship, before taking the express subway to Harlem in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem.

memes.label: Speakeasy
memes.description: An illicit establishment selling alcoholic beverages during Prohibition (1920–1933), ranging from basement dives to elegant private clubs, forming the legal-economic backdrop of Harlem's entire nightlife scene in Home to Harlem

memes.label: Spring in Harlem
memes.description: The arrival of spring transforms Harlem — trees bloom on Seventh Avenue, the community surges with new life, and Jake experiences a day of lyrical desire, street violence, music, and romantic reunion.

memes.label: Strawberry Lips
memes.description: A Harlem man — "burnt-cork black" with salmon-pink lips — whose nickname and stage-Negro appearance make him the novel's embodiment of the minstrel type that McKay both deploys and subverts

memes.label: Strivers' Row
memes.description: A prestigious block of Renaissance-style townhouses on West 138th and 139th Streets between Seventh and Eighth Avenues — the architectural embodiment of Black middle-class aspiration in 1920s Harlem

memes.label: Sweetman
memes.description: A period slang term appearing in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem, denoting a man who is financially supported by a woman — a kept man or gigolo — a status that characters alternately aspire to, accuse each other of, and disclaim.

memes.label: "Syncopation"
memes.description: "A fundamental musical technique of jazz and ragtime referenced in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem, emblematic of the rhythmic innovation that defined the Harlem Renaissance."

memes.label: Tammany Hall
memes.description: The powerful New York City Democratic political machine that, according to Claude McKay's Home to Harlem, provided protection to Harlem cabarets including the Baltimore.

memes.label: "The Baltimore"
memes.description: "A Harlem cabaret and nightclub prominently featured in Home to Harlem, where Jake meets and becomes entangled with the singer Rose."

memes.label: The Baltimore
memes.description: Harlem's premier cabaret in Home to Harlem — a Black-owned nightclub on Lenox Avenue with Tammany Hall protection, whose closure by an "indirect raid" is the novel's central public event, redistributing Harlem's nightlife from public cabarets to private buffet flats.

memes.label: The Chef
memes.description: The tyrannical dining-car chef of Chapter XII — "the most hated chef in the dining-car service," a brutal, rhinoceros-like figure who bullies the waiters until they finally "tree" him in a collective act of resistance

memes.label: The Chicago Defender
memes.description: An influential African American weekly newspaper, referenced in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem when Ray buys a copy in Pittsburgh along with other Negro publications.

memes.label: The Color Complex
memes.description: The system of intraracial color hierarchy and discrimination within the Black community of 1920s Harlem, privileging lighter skin tones over darker ones.

memes.label: The Congo
memes.description: An all-Black Harlem cabaret — "African in spirit and color" — where Rose sings, Jake socializes, and no white patrons are admitted, representing Black cultural autonomy in 1920s Harlem nightlife.

memes.label: Lodgers
memes.description: Boarding house residents in Harlem, referred to as "gentlemen lodgers" in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem, whose lives and gossip form part of the novel's social fabric. (017-015)

memes.label: The Negro World
memes.description: A weekly newspaper founded by Marcus Garvey's Universal Negro Improvement Association, referenced in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem when Ray buys a copy in Pittsburgh.

memes.label: The New Negro
memes.description: Alain Locke's landmark 1925 anthology of Harlem Renaissance writing, art, and essays — purchased by Ray on the railroad, marking his connection to the era's Black intellectual ferment in contrast to Jake's experiential engagement with Harlem culture

memes.label: The Railroad
memes.description: The Pennsylvania Railroad dining car as workplace, microcosm, and narrative center of Chapters X–XII — a site of Black male labor, racial hierarchy, intellectual awakening, and the mobility that structures the Great Migration geography of Home to Harlem

memes.label: The Sporting World
memes.description: The interconnected network of gamblers, entertainers, nightlife entrepreneurs, and pleasure-seekers that constituted Harlem's informal economy and social elite in Home to Harlem

memes.label: Tickling Blues
memes.description: A popular blues song referenced in Claude McKay's *Home to Harlem*, played on the Victrola in Harlem's buffet flats and dance halls (006-077).

memes.label: Toussaint Louverture
memes.description: Haitian revolutionary leader (c. 1743–1803) who led the only successful slave revolt in modern history — discussed at length when Ray educates Jake about Haiti on the train to Pittsburgh in Home to Harlem

memes.label: Treeing of the Chef
memes.description: The dining-car waiters' successful rebellion against their tyrannical chef — a key moment of worker solidarity and cunning in the novel's railroad chapters.

memes.label: Uncle Doc
memes.description: Proprietor of a saloon on Lenox Avenue that serves as a social hub for longshoremen and working-class Black men in Home to Harlem.

memes.label: Vaudeville
memes.description: The popular entertainment form that launched the careers of many Black performers including Bert Williams, George Walker, and Aida Overton Walker — figures remembered by characters in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem. (021-007)

memes.label: Virginia
memes.description: A Southern state referenced in Home to Harlem as a place of origin in characters' backgrounds, associated with adolescent memory and the broader Great Migration pattern that brought Black Southerners to Harlem. Also the namesake of "Virginia Dare" wine, a popular drink in Harlem's nightlife.

memes.label: Virginia Dare
memes.description: A golden wine brand referenced throughout Claude McKay's *Home to Harlem*, popularly consumed in Harlem's cabarets and buffet flats — "in those days the favorite wine of the Belt" (019-081).

memes.label: Jake's Voyage from Brest to Harlem
memes.description: Jake Brown's postwar journey from Brest, France, through London, back to Harlem — a migration that opens the novel and establishes its geography of Black Atlantic movement

memes.label: W. E. B. Du Bois
memes.description: Scholar, activist, and editor of The Crisis — the leading intellectual of the Harlem Renaissance whose criticism of Home to Harlem ignited a defining debate over art and racial representation

memes.label: Waiter
memes.description: Occupational role central to the novel's labor politics — Jake works as a dining-car waiter on the Pennsylvania Railroad, and waiters populate Harlem's cabarets and clubs. The occupation represents both economic survival and sites of collective labor resistance.

memes.label: Washington
memes.description: Washington, D.C., the American capital, which appears in the novel as a site of respectable Black middle-class life and a point of contrast with Harlem's working-class culture.

memes.label: West India
memes.description: The West India Dock district of London, a key port area where Jake works as a docker and which serves as a hub for Black seamen and workers in the novel's transatlantic world.

memes.label: West India Docks
memes.description: A historic dock complex in London where Jake Brown worked as a docker in Claude McKay's Home to Harlem, representing his life abroad between military desertion and returning home.

memes.label: Wiley Avenue
memes.description: The main Black commercial and residential street in Pittsburgh's Hill District — the long, gray, uphill street where Jake experiences the industrial bleakness of the city during his railroad layover in Home to Harlem

memes.label: World War I
memes.description: The global conflict (1914–1918) that frames the opening of Home to Harlem, shaping Jake Brown's military service, desertion, and return migration

memes.label: Yaller Prince
memes.description: A Harlem cabaret figure whose nickname encodes the intersection of color consciousness and status in 1920s nightlife — "yaller" (yellow/light-skinned) signifying desirability within the period's pigmentocracy

memes.label: Zeddy Plummer
memes.description: Jake's childhood friend from Virginia and fellow longshoreman — a stocky, volatile denizen of Harlem's sporting world whose rivalry with Jake over women and status dramatizes the fragility of male solidarity in the novel's working-class social economy
